data.title: Oracle Q3 Preview: Analysts see stable cloud infra growth, Stargate remains in focus
data.description:
Oracle (NYSE:ORCL) is expected to post an over 8% rise in its third-quarter revenue on Monday, March 10 with analysts seeing stable growth in cloud infrastructure with the Q3 print dominated by comments around the Stargate Project.

In the previous quarter, the company had reported figures below the analysts’ estimates across most key metrics including EPS, total revenue and cloud revenue.

The Stargate Project, announced at the beginning of the year, would involve close collaboration between Oracle, NVIDIA (NVDA), and OpenAI to build and operate the computing system. UBS analysts expect some insight this quarter regarding the potential financial impact of the project, which it says could be a positive catalyst.

The United States’ $500B AI initiative aims to build data centers across the country in four years, starting in Texas.

“Two separate checks (both part of the Stargate data center team) told us that the first OpenAI Abilene GPU cluster (estimated at ~130,000 GPUs) will go live in May. This implies a material potential lift to Oracle’s OCI segment beginning in the 1Q/Aug quarter, as well as the potential for a large OpenAI commitment impacting the backlog as early as the 3Q/Feb quarter,” UBS said.

Both UBS and Jefferies have maintained their Buy rating on the stock. Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts are also bullish on the ORCL and rated the stock a Buy. However, Seeking Alpha’s quant ratings recommend to Hold Oracle, largely dragged down by its growth and valuation factor.

Over the last three months, EPS estimates have seen no upward revisions, compared to 27 downward revisions, while revenue estimates have seen one upward revision versus 23 downward moves.

In the past one year, ORCL has gained nearly 32%, outperforming the nearly 11% rise in the S&P500 Index. On a year-to-date basis, however, it has lost a little over 9%.

data.title: U.S. stocks churn after jobs report, drive toward weekly loss
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages seesawed Friday afternoon, but remained on course for losses for the week, during which investors wrestled with multiple tariff announcements from the Trump administration. 

The S&P 500 500 (SP500) and the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) were each up 0.2% after losing more than 1% earlier. The Dow (DJI) +0.2%. The Nasdaq-100 (NDX) turned up +0.3% after dipping into correction territory. 

U.S. President Donald Trump during the session said reciprocal tariffs on Canadian dairy and lumber products may be announced as early as Friday. This week, he halted tariffs imposed on certain goods from Canada and Mexico until early April. 

Stocks fell sharply earlier Friday after February U.S. nonfarm payrolls rose less than expected. In bonds, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell 3 basis points to 4.24%. The 2-year yield (US2Y) fell 4 basis points to 3.92%.

Equities were set for big losses for the week that rounded off with the February nonfarm payrolls. The S&P 500 (SP500) was looking at ~3% weekly decline. 

"The week confirmed that tariffs remain a wildcard for market confidence, and traders are exhausted from the back-and-forth," Mark Malek, chief investment officer at Siebert Financial, said in a note. Markets have moved "painfully in the red" even after U.S. President Donald Trump this week delayed some tariffs on goods from Canada and Mexico for a month, Malek said. 

The February jobs report showed additions of +151K, below the 160K consensus. The unemployment rate edged up to 4.1% and average weekly wage growth increased 0.3%, cooling from January. 

The "markets should breathe a sigh of relief that there wasn’t a shock in either direction and the report was a mixed bag. The takeaway is that wage data declined which is disinflationary," Gina Bolvin, president of Bolvin Wealth Management Group, said. 

Traders on Friday raised the odds of a June interest rate cut by the Fed and pared the probability of a May reduction. 

In equities, HP Enterprise (HPE) -15% following a lower-than expected outlook stemming from tariffs and competitive pricing. 

Broadcom (AVGO) +7% after the semiconductor and software giant reported first-quarter results and guidance that topped expectations.

data.title: Funko's soft guidance rattles investors
data.description:
Funko (NASDAQ:FNKO) is in the red for the ninth time in ten days with a new 7-month low after the company’s outlook for sluggish sales of its novelty collectibles in Q1 and FY25 spooked investors and drove shares down another 8% on Friday.

As the company focuses on the execution of its strategic growth plan which revolves around financial discipline and diversification, Funko (NASDAQ:FNKO) expects “measured” top-line growth in 2025, weighted towards the second half of the year.

This translates into first quarter sales of $188M to $198M and an adjusted net loss of $0.48 to $0.40 per share which compares to Q1 2024 results of $215.7M in sales and a loss of $0.17 per share, as well as a deceleration from Q4.

For the full year, Funko (NASDAQ:FNKO) is forecasting sales to be between $1.05B to $1.082B, unchanged to slightly lower than in 2024 and below the consensus estimate of $1.09B.

With approximately a third of Funko’s (FNKO) global product purchases coming from China, this guidance assumes 20% tariffs on imports from China, as well as weakening consumer behavior in the U.S. market.

“Similar to others in our industry, we’re experiencing softening consumer behavior in the U.S. market,” Funko CEO Cynthia Williams acknowledged on the company’s earnings call with analysts, singling out tariffs and inflation for heightened concern.

For the reported quarter, a 10.6% gain in the sale of its Pop! collectibles helped offset double-digit losses for Loungefly and all other collectibles, resulting in a 0.9% increase in total sales to $293.7M, $7.6M better than expected. The company also earned a profit of $0.08 per share, which beat expectations to break even.

data.title: Chinese telecoms questioned by U.S. lawmakers over military ties, links to Beijing
data.description:
A panel of leaders from the U.S. House of Representatives has asked Chinese telecom companies to provide details of any ties to the Chinese military and government due to national security risks from their presence in America, Reuters reported Friday, citing letters viewed by the news outlet.

Democrat representative Raja Krishnamoorthi and Republican John Moolenaar have asked China Mobile, China Telecom (OTCPK:CHJHF), and China Unicom (OTCPK:CHUFF) to answer a series of questions by March 31, the report said.

U.S. lawmakers expressed concerns that Chinese entities are exploiting American data through their cloud and internet businesses and possibly providing it to Beijing. The report said the letters show growing bipartisan concern about the Chinese telecoms' U.S. footprint following a series of high-profile Chinese-led attacks on American telecoms infrastructure.

"China Telecom’s ongoing U.S. operations—particularly in internet backbone exchanges and cloud computing environments—could ... allow unauthorized data access, espionage, or sabotage by the Chinese Communist Party," the lawmakers wrote in one of the letters to the firms seen by Reuters.

The firm's "documented connections to (Chinese) intelligence raise urgent national security questions in light of the Chinese government’s increasingly aggressive attacks on U.S. telecommunications networks," they added.

In September, U.S. investigators' findings showed that a group called "Salt Typhoon," linked to China, conducted a hacking campaign and gained access to a handful of U.S. internet service providers.

They managed to burrow into broadband networks and aimed to establish a foothold within the infrastructure of cable and broadband providers, allowing them to access data stored by telecommunications companies or launch a cyberattack in the U.S.

Top Democrat on the Senate Intelligence Committee, Mark Warner, called it “the worst telecom hack in our nation’s history."

U.S. authorities last year thwarted a Chinese hacking group, "Flax Typhoon," and disrupted their network of more than 200,000 routers, cameras, and other internet-connected consumer devices that served as an entry point into U.S. networks.

"Volt Typhoon" also infiltrated into critical U.S. infrastructure systems across a wide swath of industries. Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) in 2023 said it "could disrupt critical communications infrastructure between the United States and Asia region during future crises." FBI has descibed Volt Typhoon, China’s "most significant cyber-espionage campaign in history."

Back in 2021, China Telecom Americas' authorization to provide telecommunications services in the U.S. was rejected by the Federal Communications Commission on grounds of national security. It previously had the authorization to provide services for nearly 20 years.

According to the FCC, China Telecom (OTCPK:CHJHF) had used vulnerabilities in the Border Gateway Protocol—a set of rules that determines the best path for data to travel across the internet—to "misroute United States internet traffic on at least six occasions."

In 2022, the FCC revoked China Unicom America's (OTCPK:CHUFF) authorization to operate in the U.S., and also rejected China Mobile's bid to provide U.S. telecommunications services in 2019.

Nothing prevents Chinese telecoms from providing cloud services and routing wholesale U.S. internet traffic, which gives them access to Americans' data, the lawmakers said.

Chinese hackers had also targeted data from phones used by President Donald Trump and VP JD Vance during their election campaign trail.

T-Mobile (NASDAQ:TMUS), Verizon (NYSE:VZ), AT&T (NYSE:T) Charter Communications (NASDAQ:CHTR), Fortinet (NASDAQ:FTNT), and Cisco (NASDAQ:CSCO) were impacted by the Chinese hacking campaigns. China has denied all allegations.

data.title: Equal weight S&P 500 outperforms in 2025 as market-cap weighted index declines
data.description:
While much of the focus is on the broader S&P 500 (SP500) index, which is currently down for the year due to ongoing concerns about inflation, tariffs, and the latest jobs report, it's worth noting that the equal weight S&P 500 index has remained in positive territory for 2025, albeit by a small margin.

As of 2025, the S&P 500 is down 2.9% year-to-date and is currently 6.5% below its all-time high, with the index hovering around 5,740. In contrast, the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (NYSEARCA:RSP) has gained 0.4% year-to-date, trading near $175.85 per share.

This suggests that while the overall market may be struggling, the performance of smaller-cap stocks within the index are showing some level of resilience, offering a different perspective on the market's health amidst these economic challenges.

In terms of where the market may move from here, JPMorgan stated: “In the near-term, we expect range bound S&P 500 (5200-6000) with still high spot stock dispersion, but for the market to restrengthen later this year. We maintain our year end price target of 6500 but acknowledge that there is a large standard error around this forecast and the possibility that S&P 500 may not reach this level until 2026.”

Investors looking to stay informed on the S&P 500 may consider tracking both index ETFs and mutual funds. Below is a list of options to consider:

S&P 500 Tracking Funds: (MUTF:FXAIX), (MUTF:VFIAX), (MUTF:VFFSX), (MUTF:SWPPX), (NYSEARCA:SPY), (NYSEARCA:VOO), (NYSEARCA:IVV), (NYSEARCA:RSP), (NYSEARCA:SSO), (NYSEARCA:UPRO), (NYSEARCA:SH), (NYSEARCA:SDS) and (NYSEARCA:SPXU).

data.title: The early adopters of AI productivity and their use cases – Goldman
data.description:
Goldman Sachs analysts put together a list of companies that have discussed already adopting artificial intelligence and that have identified productivity benefits during their fourth quarter earnings calls.

These stocks are constituents of an early “fourth phase” of the AI adoption process.

Ryan Hammond, vice president of U.S. Equity Strategy at Goldman Sachs, said that specific use cases included customer support, coding and engineering, and supply chain and inventory management.

According to several surveys, we are still in the early stages of widespread AI adoption, said Hammound. A U.S. Census Bureau survey showed that 5% of companies are using AI in the production of goods and services, but a recent Federal Reserve paper showed that the share has increased to 20% with some adjustments.

The following are early AI productivity adopters – S&P 500 (SP500) companies that offered on their fourth quarter earnings calls specific examples of how they are using AI to enhance productivity internally.

For operations, examples include (AIG), (BK), (C), (NDAQ), (MCK), (UNH), (TMUS), (F), (KMX), (BR), (GWW), (LDOS), (RTX), (CTSH).

For customer support, examples include (CHTR), (T), (ABNB), (AMZN), (RCL), (EBAY), (CNC), (FDX), (PAYC), (IT), (PSA).

For engineering and coding, (GOOGL), (BKNG), (EXPE), (WMT), (SPGI), (CNC), (HCA), (GD), (GDDY).

For supply chain and inventory management, (AMZN), (RL), (EL), (ADP), (WMT), (TGT).

For employee efficiency, (CHTR), (MSCI), (SPGI), (DGX), (PAYC), (PAYX), (CRWD), (ENPH), (MSFT), (GDDY), (IT), (PSA).

For marketing purposes, (EXPE), (KO), (TGT).

And for general operations, examples included (TMUS), (EBAY), (F), (KMX), (AIG), (V), (C), (AMP), (MCK), (UNH), (BR), (GWW), (JCI), (RTX), (DELL), (GDDY), (INTU), (SNPS), (NRG), (SLB), (CVX), (OXY).

data.title: U.S. tariffs on Canada dairy, lumber may be on the way, Trump says
data.description:
Canada may be hit with reciprocal tariffs on its dairy and lumber products as soon as Friday, U.S. President Donald Trump said. 

Speaking in the Oval Office, Trump said U.S. dairy products carry a 250% export levy into Canada, and that tariffs are high on U.S. lumber. 

“They make it impossible for us to sell lumber or dairy products into Canada, but our numbers are a tiny fraction of that, almost nonexistent,” Trump said. “They'll be met with the exact same tariff unless they drop it, and that's what reciprocal means,” he said. “And we may do it as early as today,” or on Monday or Tuesday, he said. 

The Trump administration has been teeing up an April 2 announcement on reciprocal, or tit-for-tat, tariffs for U.S. trading partners. Softwood lumber has been an area of trade contention between the U.S. and Canada for years. 

Trump’s remarks Friday come after his decisions this week to, for one month, halt tariffs on some goods under the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA, trade deal, including cars and auto parts. 

In markets Friday, lumber futures (LB1:COM) rose 1% to $639 per thousand board feet, coming off session lows in late-morning trade. The iShares Global Timber & Forestry ETF (WOOD) rose 0.5%, also coming off a weaker price. 

U.S. stocks (SP500)(VOO)(COMP:IND)(DJI) on Friday seesawed and faced weekly losses. Canada's TSX Index (SPTSX) gained 0.5%. 

U.S. lumber futures (LB1:COM) early this week hit 2022 highs after Trump told the Commerce Department to investigate potential harm to U.S. national security posed by lumber imports.

data.title: Trump plans to restrict student loan forgiveness in non-profit jobs - report
data.description:
President Donald Trump plans to rescind student loan forgiveness for borrowers who work for non-governmental organizations that engage in "improper activities" tied to such things as illegal immigration and terrorist groups, according to a media report.

White House Staff Secretary Will Scharf told Bloomberg News that Trump expects to sign an executive order to direct the Department of Education and the Treasury Department to modify the program to rescind eligibility.

In 2007, the government created a program to full forgive student loans for borrowers in public service jobs, such as teaching, nursing and public safety, if they made payments on the loans for at least 10 years and stayed in the profession.

However, before President Joe Biden took office only 7,000 public service workers received any form of debt cancellation through the program, Bloomberg said.
